title: Keynote Address from Eric Lander
text:
thank you sue thank you for really your
leadership in organizing
today's program
well it's a great privilege for me of
course to welcome eric lander
a good friend
and
president biden's chief science advisor
and director of white house office of
science and technology policy
now eric
is the keynote speaker for our meeting
but
as he's always so creative
he decided that he and i should have a
five-sided chat
and so i would be happy to do this with
him but let me first introduce him
as i said he's president biden's science
advisor and director of ostp
but he is formerly the president and
founding director of the bold institute
we all know that well a research
institute that's closely affiliated with
mit harvard and five of boston major
teaching hospital believe me that's
quite a feat
and is able to bring that together to
focus on genomic medicine
now eric was one of the principal
leaders of human genome project
and as you know he's a geneticist
molecular biologist and a mathematician
that's where he started his career
he's played a pioneering role in reading
understanding and biomedical application
of a human genome
and and he's developing developed
powerful methods
for discovering the molecular basis of
human disease that are used all over the
world
and
no surprises he's received numerous
national
and international honors for his work
now eric has been a scientific advisor
to federal government across multiple
administrations
in fact he was peak house co-chair
to obama president obama
so he has advised not only the white
house but hhs and department of defense
so eric welcome
good morning
oh hello victor can you see me
we can see you in your blue shirt and
all right
excellent the thing you left out victor
is i'm also a member of the national
academy of medicine
and um
i i think it's relevant and it's why i
thought we should do a fireside or a
keynote conversation because you know i
am coming as the president of science
advisor and all that but i'm i'm coming
as a member of the national academy of
medicine of course
uh because it's because it's a community
i i know and love and i just want to say
how much i appreciate the nam has been
doing throughout the pandemic and
thinking hard about questions related to
public health and climate and so it's
great it's great to keep off a
conversation with you and i i hear you
might even take a couple of questions
from the membership as well yes we will
do that as well
so i think what you should do is
look at our conversation three parts of
the totally
you know interrelated
first of all i'm going to ask you a
question about how you sign and see
signs
forward in the future
and secondly i want you to talk a little
bit more about
pandemic preparedness and public health
and third about climate i know these are
all things that you're working on and
peak house is working on
so eric in 1944
president franklin roosevelt authored a
letter to a science advisor
dr vanever bush he asked the question
how science and technology could be best
applied to benefit nations health
economic personality and national
security in decades that follow the
second world war
and as you know dr bush response came in
form of a seminal report called science
the endless frontier
that would set the course of scientific
discovery in america for the next 75
years
and i just want to read a quote from
what he wrote he said the most important
way in which the government can promote
industrial research are to increase the
flow of new scientific knowledge
through support of basic research and to
aid in development of scientific talent
well you all know this christian report
was a guiding force for changes
in science and innovation in our country
for decades
that led to the modernization american
research university
the creation of nsf and later nih and
many others and shape much
science policy
so much has happened
and of course when you
were appointed at least
selected shall we say and they're later
confirmed as
the science advisor to president biden
he wrote you a similar letter on january
15
and ask you about
five questions
questions about what can we learn about
pandemic
and address the needs of public health
and we come to that as kind of second
part of our conversation
how can breakthrough science technology
create powerful new solutions to address
climate change
that's our third topic
and here he's saying
how can we assure united states is world
leader in technology industry of the
future
also critical to our economic
personality
national security and competition
in which case he listed china
so i want you to talk about that and of
course
how can you guarantee the fruits of
science technology can be shared across
america
among all americans and what is the
long-term health of science technology
animation so eric
75 years what next 75 years would look
like
well victor thank you for uh calling
attention to this wonderful letter that
president biden sent
it's formally addressed to me
but it's actually addressed to the
entire community of science and
technology and i don't mean just people
who happen to have degrees in or work in
jobs of science and technology but
people who care about it so
you know the president entrusted me with
with providing an answer back to him but
it's it's all of us who have to think
about these questions so
you said that veneever bush
produced this amazing report science the
endless frontier
and he called attention to the
importance of the centrality in our
strategy of
continued commitment to producing new
scientific knowledge and new talent and
of course that's going to have to
continue
but you're asking well what goes on in
the next 75 years well
for one thing
um
the science itself i think
it just has so much possibility right
now there is so much that we look ahead
that we couldn't even imagine
a couple of decades ago i'll give a
couple of examples but i won't mostly
talk about scientific possibilities but
i can help it can't help emphasizing it
to say how much science continues to
transform itself and that this
fundamental frame that geneva bush set
out is correct you know i think about in
in the field of biology and medicine
the
the
the transition
of biology to being a digital science
with digital information
is now
leading to the the idea of programmable
biology programmable medicine
this is a really important
transformation that's been happening
over the coming decades you know from
being able to read out the the
genome the digital information that
encodes the genes and all of that
information to then more recently
reading out all the variation in the
human populations and then reading out
how the genes are read out the rna
expression patterns and all the cell
types in the body is going on to then
being able to read out and understand
regulatory programs we're not there yet
but we're gonna we're gonna get to a
deep
quote maybe comprehensive understanding
about how to read out regulation
and then what will happen is and is
beginning to happen already that people
will think about how do you program
cells how do you put in a patch to the
code that might do something to help you
know make a cell that's become insulin
insensitive become more insulin
sensitive
um you can think about doing that you
know if you're thinking about this in a
digital digital way you can think about
it with nucleic acids carrying
instructions anywhere from the temporary
and rna to a more permanent sort of
thing
and
we will see people who write code for
cells we see it already in this pandemic
with mrna vaccines the idea that one can
sit down rather rapidly
and write code and one can ask can i do
this for cancer could i make cancer
vaccines what else could be done
with digital reading can i do cancer
diagnostics
in quite general ways with reading
things out say from annual blood tests
they're just a swirl of ideas
that i think even those of us who are
close to the world of genomics
never could have predicted just how far
those ideas would go
and they'll go even further than what i
can imagine today because of this basic
investment in fundamental knowledge and
talent so i'm excited about that and
outside our own fields you know just in
the past several months i'm excited
about things like fusion energy
something that was talked about since i
was an undergraduate student
well that was really getting legs there
are dozens of startups in fusion who
might actually make working fusion
reactors that really could contribute so
there is no end to this endless frontier
i think we've confirmed the endlessness
of this frontier
but we could also think about what did
geneva bush not get in that report what
did he not think of and what did he
maybe you know well just really what did
he not think of i think there are a lot
of things that have to be said and they
really go to the heart of what president
biden has asked us all to do which is to
reimagine and reinvigorate
the science and technology strategy for
the next 75 years give or take to the
end of this century
and what one of the first is geneva's
veneer bush's
vision of science was a very elitist
vision it was a vision
of work done at a very limited number of
elite institutions largely done by the
kind of scientists he knew which were
largely white males scientists working
in in these kinds of institutions
and um
i think it's clear we're not going to
succeed unless we really open up that
vision dramatically because science
hasn't been welcoming to to people with
regard to gender to race
to having the resources to go into
science
and with regard to region science is
still very regionally biased into where
it takes place
highly concentrated in the northeast and
the west coast
and when president biden writes how do
we ensure the participation
of all americans
in science itself and in the benefits of
science um we really need to take that
seriously because it's both the right
thing to do but it's going to be
essential to our succeeding as a nation
and so we need people around the table
participating in science we're going to
ask
a wide variety of different questions
and bring different perspectives and i
think this is one of the single most
important things we have to do is take
seriously that this has to be a whole of
country all americans from all
backgrounds i know this is something the
national academy of medicine thinks a
lot about but i just want to emphasize
that at the white house this has become
a very major focus as well and we are
hoping early in the new year to come out
with
some uh really specific things we can do
to help accelerate this i think other
things that we're not as apparent to
beneath bush we need to take on
collective problems that require global
cooperation
um issues like solving pandemics can't
be done in a in a city alone in a
country alone it takes a world working
together climate takes a world working
together this was not the case in the
time of franklin roosevelt our problems
are so much more interconnected
and that is going to require thinking
about science differently they also
involve scale that is different
an awful lot of the the early nsf that
geneva bush conceived was about
individual investigators at the bench we
still need that it's obviously the life
of love for sure but we now need to
understand how to build great portfolios
of projects of all different sizes
because we can't be afraid to take on
larger scale problems if that's what
it's going to take and we have to
preserve individual creativity even
within the large scale problems exactly
so there are there are important things
about the sociology here and industry
has become an important font of
discovery and we have to think about the
spectrum across academia to industry in
terms of what can be done and ensuring
the openness of all this these
vaniva bush's world was a simpler world
with regard to these things much of the
science took place on your lab bench or
in your exactly right so you know also i
gotta say in 1944 and the post-year wars
post-war years
the us was sort of the only game in town
with regard to science and technology
there was no serious competition for the
united states well we're now in a world
where everybody has read the playbook
and many countries are actively
participating in science and technology
and there's even serious competition in
in many ways in science and technology
and for the united states to succeed we
need to think about how we ensure that
we succeed on a global stage by of
course including everybody within the us
but also thinking about the alliances
making sure that we view this as not
just team usa but reaching out and
working closely with allies and having
having it be easy for people to come
into the us to do science and to share
ideas and projects
and uh you know the united states has to

for scientists all over the world
to come and practice science we train
and do science i note that of the nobel

of the americans who receive nobel
prizes all but one was an immigrant to
this country
and so
we are stronger and the world is
stronger when we keep open those those
borders and maybe the last thing i'll
say on this picture for this next 75
years this is still a work in progress
and i want to hear from everybody about
what we should say to president biden
about the strategy but but something
else that i don't think benevo bush
thought enough about at the time
was
public trust
the need to earn public trust maybe
after world war ii it wasn't much of an
issue the public just trusted scientists
for a lot of reasons including sometimes

through
misinformation we really have serious
issues about trust trust is going to
require us approaching this with
humility we can't just say oh the public
doesn't get it we have to first ask are
we truly including are we truly making
sure that science benefits all are we
speaking clearly and we need to earn
that trust every day and we need to
bring a humility and a persistence to it
so these are some of my initial thoughts
about the response to president biden
they will get more refined and will
actually write something at some point
but i don't want to do it too soon
before we hear from everybody so this
part's an open invitation to everybody
to join enemies
